docs.title: Wiki Index
docs.text:
A personal knowledge base on nutrition science for seniors over 75.

Last updated: 2026-04-15 (updated after third and fourth ingest)

docs.title: Source Summaries
docs.text: Page Description [[gut-health-guide-zoe-2024]] ZOE's 2024 practical guide: five steps to improve gut health, with recipes and a glossary [[are-all-upfs-equally-bad]] ZOE 2025 article: not all UPFs carry equal risk; introduces the ZOE Processed Food Risk Scale [[gut-health-guide-zoe-uk]] UK edition of ZOE's Gut Health Made Easy guide; same five steps but with an expanded shopping list [[better-breakfast-guide-zoe]] ZOE's 2025 guide to gut-friendly breakfasts; six recipes, five science-approved swaps, and key statistics

docs.title: Concept Pages
docs.text: Page Description [[gut-microbiome]] The community of trillions of microbes in the digestive system; central to gut and overall health [[plant-diversity]] The 30-plants-a-week principle; why variety of plant foods matters for the microbiome [[dietary-fibre]] Indigestible carbohydrate that feeds gut bacteria and supports bowel regularity [[fermented-foods]] Foods changed by beneficial bacteria or yeasts; a direct source of probiotic organisms [[polyphenols]] Colour-providing plant compounds that may promote beneficial gut bacteria growth [[ultra-processed-foods]] Manufactured foods with artificial ingredients linked to negative gut health effects [[gut-friendly-recipes]] Five practical gut-friendly recipes from the ZOE gut health guide [[nova-classification]] The four-group food processing framework widely used in research, and its limitations [[zoe-processed-food-risk-scale]] ZOE's five-tier scale rating processed foods by health impact, not processing level [[breakfast-recipes]] Six gut-friendly breakfast recipes from ZOE's Better Breakfast Guide [[blood-sugar]] How breakfast composition affects blood sugar stability, energy, and hunger throughout the day

docs.title: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health? (ZOE, 2025)
docs.text:
Summary: A ZOE article by Tim Newman, reviewed by Dr Federica Amati, arguing that not all ultra-processed foods carry the same health risk and introducing ZOE's Processed Food Risk Scale as an alternative to the NOVA classification.

Sources: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md

Last updated: 2026-04-14

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Published April 2025 on zoe.com and reviewed by Federica Amati PhD, MPH, RNutr, this article draws on several large epidemiological studies to argue that the health impact of [[ultra-processed-foods]] depends on the type of UPF, not simply its level of processing (source: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md).

It introduces the [[nova-classification]] system, explains its limitations, and presents the [[zoe-processed-food-risk-scale]] as a more nuanced replacement.

docs.title: Key argument
docs.text: While overall UPF consumption is linked to poorer health outcomes, individual UPF categories behave very differently in research. Some are consistently harmful; others appear neutral or even beneficial (source: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md).

docs.title: Cardiovascular disease (200,000+ participants, US)
docs.text:
• Total UPF intake linked to 17% increased cardiovascular disease risk over three decades.
  • Higher-risk UPFs: sugar-sweetened drinks, artificially-sweetened drinks, processed meats.
  • Lower-risk UPFs: bread, cold cereals, yoghurt/dairy desserts, savoury snacks.

docs.title: Type 2 diabetes (200,000 participants)
docs.text:
• Highest UPF consumers had 46% increased type 2 diabetes risk over up to 30 years.
  • Higher-risk UPFs: refined breads, sauces/condiments, artificially/sugar-sweetened drinks, animal-based products, ready-to-eat meals.
  • Lower-risk UPFs: cereals, dark/wholegrain breads, packaged snacks, fruit-based products, yoghurt/dairy desserts.

docs.title: Type 2 diabetes (311,892 participants, 2024)
docs.text:
• Each 10% increase in daily UPFs associated with 17% higher type 2 diabetes risk over 10 years.
  • Higher-risk: savoury snacks, animal-based products, ready meals, sweetened drinks.
  • Lower-risk: breads, biscuits, breakfast cereals, plant-based alternatives.

docs.title: Why do UPF types differ in risk?
docs.text:
The article identifies four factors that explain why some UPFs are more harmful than others (source: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md):

  1. Chemical additives — colorants, emulsifiers, and preservatives may affect the [[gut-microbiome]].
  2. High energy, sugar, saturated fat, and salt — independently harmful regardless of processing.
  3. Hyperpalatability and energy density — easy to over-consume; 38% of foods in ZOE's PREDICT data were both energy-dense and hyperpalatable.
  4. Displacement of whole foods — eating more UPFs leaves less room for foods that support health.

Some UPFs, including bread, cereals, and popcorn, can contain [[dietary-fibre]], minerals, and [[polyphenols]], and may not carry the above risks.

docs.title: ZOE's practical guidance
docs.text:
• 80/20 rule: Aim for 80% whole foods, 20% more convenient options.
  • It is "pretty much impossible" to avoid all UPFs; focus on cutting the highest-risk ones consumed most often.
  • See [[zoe-processed-food-risk-scale]] for the full five-tier system.

docs.title: Contradiction with earlier source
docs.text: The ZOE Gut Health Guide 2024 ([[gut-health-guide-zoe-2024]]) frames UPFs in a broadly negative light without distinguishing between categories. This 2025 article adds important nuance: the same organisation now explicitly states that some UPFs are neutral or beneficial. The practical advice (limit the worst, allow balance) is consistent across both sources.

docs.title: Better Breakfast Guide (Zoe, 2025)
docs.text:
Summary: ZOE's 2025 guide to gut-friendly breakfasts; six recipes, five science-approved swaps, statistics on common breakfast mistakes, and guidance on the importance of morning meal composition for blood sugar stability.

Sources: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf

Last updated: 2026-04-15

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This guide, published by ZOE in 2025, argues that most people start the day with a breakfast that is high in sugar and refined carbohydrates, low in protein and fibre, and missing opportunities for gut-supporting plant diversity. It offers practical fixes using simple swaps and easy-recipes.

docs.title: Key statistics
docs.text:
• 58% of Americans start the day with too much sugar (source: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf).
  • 80% don't eat enough fibre at breakfast (source: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf).
  • 70% miss out on fermented foods (source: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf).
  • 60% don't get enough protein (source: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf).

docs.title: Why breakfast matters
docs.text: Breakfast composition affects [[blood-sugar]] stability for the rest of the day. A high-sugar, low-protein breakfast produces a rapid blood sugar spike followed by a crash, leading to hanger and fatigue. A balanced breakfast with protein, fat, and [[dietary-fibre]] produces a gentler rise and a more steady energy curve.

docs.title: The four elements of a good breakfast
docs.text:
ZOE recommends that a gut-friendly breakfast includes (source: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf):

  1. Fibre -- from oats, berries, seeds, or nuts; to feed beneficial gut bacteria.
  2. Protein -- from eggs, yoghurt, cottage cheese, or legumes; to slow digestion and stabilise [[blood-sugar]].
  3. Healthy fats -- from avocado, nuts, or olive oil; to support satiety and nutrient absorption.
  4. Plant diversity -- an opportunity to add to the [[plant-diversity]] target of 30 plants a week.

docs.title: Five science-approved breakfast swaps
docs.text:
These are direct swaps recommended by ZOE's nutritionists (source: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf):

  1. Orange juice -> Whole fruit: juice removes fibre and concentrates sugar; whole oranges provide fibre and slower sugar release.
  2. Sugary cereal -> Oats or muesli: sugary cereals spike blood sugar; oats provide prebiotic fibre and sustained energy.
  3. White toast -> Sourdough or wholegrain: white bread has a high glcyemic impact; sourdough and wholegrain bread digest more slowly and contain more fibre.
  4. Flavoured yoghurt -> Plain Greek yoghurt + fruit: flavoured yoghurts are often high in added sugar; plain Greek yoghurt has more protein and live cultures (see [[fermented-foods]]).
  5. Processed meats -> Eggs or legumes: bacon and sausages are high-risk [[ultra-processed-foods]]; eggs and legumes are high-protein whole foods.

docs.title: Key nutrient targets at breakfast
docs.text:
• Protein: Aim for at least 20g (source: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf).
  • Fibre: Aim for 6–8g per breakfast (out of the 30g daily target) (source: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf).
  • Fermented foods: Include at least one serving daily, e.g. kefir, live-yoghurt, or sauerkraut (see [[fermented-foods]]) (source: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf).

docs.title: Recipes
docs.text: For the six specific breakfast recipes from this guide, see [[breakfast-recipes]].

docs.title: Notes for seniors
docs.text: This guide is especially relevant for adults over 75, as adequate protein intake at each meal supports muscle maintenance and prevents frailty. The swaps and recipes do not require complex cooking, and several can be prepared the night before (e.g. Birtcher muesli, chia pudding).

docs.title: Blood Sugar
docs.text:
Summary: How breakfast composition affects blood sugar stability, energy, and hunger throughout the day.

Sources: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf

Last updated: 2026-04-15

docs.title: What is blood sugar?
docs.text: Blood sugar (blood glucose) refers to the concentration of glucose in the bloodstream at any given time. When you eat carbohydrates, they are broken down into glucose, which enters the bloodstream. Insulin is then released by the pancreas to move glucose into cells.

docs.title: Why stability matters
docs.text:
Blood sugar spikes and crashes can lead to (source: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf):

  • Low energy and fatigue
  • Cravings for sugary or starchy foods
  • Difficulty concentrating
  • Hunger shortly after eating
  • Over-time, repeated spikes may contribute to insulin resistance and increased risk of type 2 diabetes

docs.title: The breakfast connection
docs.text: Breakfast is particularly important for blood sugar management because the composition of your morning meal sets the tone for the rest of the day. ZOE's research shows that blood sugar responses to the same food can vary significantly between individuals (source: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf).

docs.title: What causes spikes?
docs.text:
• High refined carbohydrates: white bread, sugary cereals, orange juice, flavoured yoghurt (source: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf)
  • Low protein: without protein to slow absorption, glucose enters the bloodstream quickly
  • Low fibre: [[dietary-fibre]] slows digestion and absorption; low-fibre meals produce faster sugar entry

docs.title: What stabilises blood sugar?
docs.text:
• Protein: eggs, yoghurt, cottage cheese, legumes
  • Healthy fats: avocado, nuts, olive oil
  • Fiber: oats, seeds, berries, wholegrain bread
  • Fermented foods: kefir and yoghurt may improve glucose responses (see [[fermented-foods]])
  • Vinegar or sourdough: acid (acetic acid) slows glucose absorption

docs.title: Notes for seniors
docs.text: For adults over 75, [[blood-sugar]] stability is linked not only to diabetes risk but also to cognitive function, energy levels, and mood. Stable blood sugar throughout the day may reduce fatigue, improve concentration, and support overall wellbeing. This is particularly relevant for seniors who may already have impaired insulin sensitivity.

docs.title: Breakfast Recipes (ZOE)
docs.text:
Summary: Six gut-friendly breakfast recipes from ZOE's Better Breakfast Guide, designed to provide fibre, protein, healthy fats, and plant diversity to start the day well.

Sources: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf

Last updated: 2026-04-15

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: These recipes are part of ZOE's Better Breakfast Guide. Each is designed to correct common breakfast mistakes — insufficient [[dietary-fibre]], too little protein, and high refined carbohydrates. Each recipe lists a plant count and, where shown, a ZOE App overall score.

docs.title: Recipe 1: Dark Cacao Chia Pudding
docs.text:
By: Dr. Federica Amati, ZOE Head Nutritionist

Why it's gut-friendly: Chia seeds are high in plant protein, all essential amino acids, omega-3 fatty acids, fibre, and [[polyphenols]]. Described as the "gut's favourite fat" — omega-3 fatty acids have a direct anti-inflammatory effect and the [[gut-microbiome]] is particularly fond of them.

Plant count: 2+ (depending on toppings) | ZOE score: 100 (enjoy daily)

Ingredients (serves 1): 2–3 tsp chia seeds, 1 tsp raw cacao powder, ½–¾ cup dairy milk / plant-based milk / kefir, optional frozen berries, optional honey.

Method: Add chia seeds and cacao powder to a glass jar. Pour in milk or kefir. Add berries and honey if using. Refrigerate overnight. Eat the next day.

Tips for seniors: Prepare the night before — no morning effort needed. Berries can be frozen (equally nutritious and easier to keep on hand).

docs.title: Recipe 2: Probiotic Bircher Muesli
docs.text:
Why it's gut-friendly: Oats provide prebiotic fibre (see [[dietary-fibre]]). Kefir adds live cultures (see [[fermented-foods]]). Grated apple, mixed seeds, and optional raisins contribute plant diversity. 8g fibre per serving.

Plant count: 5+ (depending on toppings) | ZOE score: 80 (enjoy regularly)

Ingredients (serves 1–2): 1 apple (grated), 100g kefir, 2 tbsp Greek yoghurt, 1 tsp cinnamon, 50g oats, 50ml milk (or extra kefir), 40g mixed seeds, optional 25g raisins.

Method: Grate apple into a large bowl. Mix in all remaining ingredients. Cover and refrigerate overnight (or at least a couple of hours). Add toppings to serve.

Suggested toppings: Fresh or frozen fruit, nuts (almonds or walnuts), seeds (chia or flax), drizzle of honey or maple syrup.

docs.title: Recipe 3: Cottage Cheese Pancakes
docs.text:
Why it's gut-friendly: Oats (complex carbohydrates) combined with cottage cheese (protein) slow sugar absorption and lead to more stable blood sugar. 27g protein per serving. Nut and berry toppings add healthy fats and fibre.

Plant count: 6+ (depending on toppings) | Cooking time: 20 minutes

Ingredients (serves 2): 50g oats, 1 banana (optional), 150g cottage cheese, 1 egg, pinch each of salt, baking powder, and cinnamon, splash of water or milk.

Method: Blend oats to a fine flour. Add banana, cottage cheese, egg, salt, baking powder, cinnamon, and liquid. Blend until smooth. Cook in a non-stick pan with a little butter or oil until golden brown on each side. Top with Greek yoghurt, frozen raspberries, walnuts, figs, and a drizzle of honey.

docs.title: Recipe 4: Breakfast Hash
docs.text:
Why it's gut-friendly: Black beans contain resistant starch that feeds gut microbes. Peppers contain flavonoids (a subclass of [[polyphenols]]) that support the gut lining. Anti-inflammatory spices (cumin, smoked paprika) add plant diversity.

Plant count: 30+ | ZOE score: 90 (enjoy daily) | Cooking time: 20–30 minutes

Ingredients (serves 2): 1 tbsp extra virgin olive oil, 1 small sweet potato (diced), 1 red pepper (chopped), ½ zucchini (diced), handful of cherry tomatoes, 1 can black beans (drained), ½ tsp smoked paprika, 1 tsp cumin, ¼ cup crumbled feta, cilantro for garnish, optional 2 eggs.

Method: Fry sweet potato in olive oil until cooked. Add pepper, zucchini, tomatoes, and spices; cook until soft. Stir in black beans for 2 minutes. Optional: crack eggs over the top and cover until whites are set. Top with feta and cilantro.

docs.title: Recipe 5: No Ordinary Avocado Toast
docs.text:
Why it's gut-friendly: White beans add protein and resistant starch. Avocado provides healthy fats. Sourdough bread has a lower blood sugar impact than white bread. Pickled red onions or sauerkraut add [[fermented-foods]].

Plant count: 30+ | Cooking time: 5–10 minutes

Ingredients (serves 2): 1 can white beans (drained), 1 ripe avocado, juice and zest of 1 lemon, ½ tsp red pepper flakes, ½ tsp za'atar, 2 slices sourdough bread, salt and black pepper, pickled red onions or sauerkraut.

Method: Mash white beans and avocado together with lemon juice, zest, salt, pepper, and red pepper flakes. Spread on toasted sourdough. Top with sauerkraut or pickled red onions and za'atar.

docs.title: Recipe 6: Cinnamon Pecan Granola (Bonus)
docs.text:
Why it's gut-friendly: Whole grains, nuts, and seeds provide fibre, healthy fats, and plant diversity. Cinnamon is an anti-inflammatory spice that contributes towards the 30-plants-a-week target.

(Full ingredient quantities not shown in source; follows the same gut-friendly principles as the other recipes.)

docs.title: Tips for Seniors Over 75
docs.text:
• Recipes 1 and 2 (chia pudding, Bircher muesli) can be prepared the evening before, requiring minimal morning effort.
  • Recipe 5 (avocado toast) takes under 10 minutes.
  • Frozen fruit and canned beans are convenient, equally nutritious alternatives to fresh.
  • All recipes can be adapted in portion size.

docs.title: Dietary Fibre
docs.text:
Summary: A type of carbohydrate found in plant foods that the body cannot digest. It supports a healthy gut microbiome and promotes regular bowel movements.

Sources: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf; 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf

Last updated: 2026-04-15

docs.title: What is dietary fibre?
docs.text:
Dietary fibre is a type of carbohydrate found in plant foods that the body cannot digest (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf). Rather than being absorbed, it passes through the digestive system, where it feeds beneficial gut bacteria and supports bowel regularity.

Fibre is one of the most important nutrients for a healthy [[gut-microbiome]].

docs.title: Prebiotics
docs.text:
A special category of fibre called prebiotics specifically helps beneficial bacteria grow in the gut (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf). Good sources of prebiotics include:

  • Oats
  • Garlic
  • Onions
  • Leeks
  • Asparagus

These foods are also featured in the ZOE gut-friendly shopping list and recipes. See [[gut-friendly-recipes]].

docs.title: How to increase fibre intake
docs.text:
The ZOE guide recommends these practical steps (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf):

  • Make high-fibre swaps (e.g. wholemeal bread instead of white)
  • Use pulses and legumes to bulk out dishes
  • Batch cook whole grains and store in the fridge
  • Keep the skin on vegetables like potatoes and squash

docs.title: High-fibre foods
docs.text:
From the ZOE shopping list (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf), good fibre sources include:

  • Beans and legumes (chickpeas, lentils, butter beans, black beans)
  • Whole grains (oats, brown rice, quinoa, rye, spelt)
  • Vegetables (especially with skins on)
  • Fruits (particularly berries, pears, apples)
  • Nuts and seeds (flaxseeds, chia seeds, pumpkin seeds)

docs.title: Resistant starch
docs.text:
Resistant starch is a type of starch that, like fibre, is not digested in the small intestine. Instead, it ferments in the large intestine, feeding beneficial gut bacteria (source: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf).

Good sources include:

  • Black beans and other legumes
  • Cooled cooked potatoes and rice
  • Oats

The ZOE Breakfast Hash recipe specifically uses black beans for their resistant starch content. See [[breakfast-recipes]].

docs.title: Fibre at breakfast
docs.text: ZOE data shows that only approximately 6% of people get enough fibre at breakfast — despite this being an important opportunity to start meeting daily fibre needs (source: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf). Fibre at breakfast also helps stabilise [[blood-sugar]] by slowing glucose absorption.

docs.title: Relevance for seniors
docs.text: Adequate fibre intake supports bowel regularity, which can be a concern for older adults. It also feeds beneficial gut bacteria, which tend to decline with age.

docs.title: Fermented Foods
docs.text:
Summary: Foods changed by beneficial bacteria or yeasts that break down starches and sugars. They are a key source of probiotic organisms that benefit the gut microbiome.

Sources: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf

Last updated: 2026-04-14

docs.title: What are fermented foods?
docs.text:
Fermented foods are foods that have been changed by beneficial bacteria or yeasts, which break down their starches and sugars (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf). This process produces live microorganisms — often called probiotics — that can benefit the [[gut-microbiome]] when consumed.

Fermented foods are distinct from [[dietary-fibre]] in that they deliver live bacteria directly, rather than feeding the bacteria already present.

docs.title: Examples of fermented foods
docs.text:
From the ZOE guide (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf):

  • Kefir — a fermented milk drink; also used in recipes such as the green goddess chickpea sandwich
  • Yoghurt — widely available and easy to incorporate into meals
  • Sauerkraut — fermented cabbage; can be made at home
  • Kimchi — fermented vegetables, typically including cabbage and chilli
  • Miso — fermented soybean paste; used in soups and dressings
  • Kombucha — fermented tea drink
  • Fermented cheese — e.g. aged cheddar; a practical option for those unfamiliar with other fermented foods
  • Pickles — fermented or brined vegetables
  • Tempeh — fermented soybeans; a good protein source

docs.title: Practical tips
docs.text:
The ZOE guide recommends (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf):

  • Add a small amount of fermented food to a meal
  • Swap half the milk in breakfast with kefir or yoghurt
  • Try fermented cheese such as aged cheddar as an easy starting point
  • Make your own sauerkraut

docs.title: Notes for seniors
docs.text: Fermented foods are generally well tolerated and easy to include in everyday meals. Yoghurt and kefir in particular are familiar and widely available. Starting with small amounts is advisable if someone is new to fermented foods.

docs.title: Gut-Friendly Recipes
docs.text:
Summary: Five practical recipes from the ZOE Gut Health guide, each designed to incorporate multiple gut-health principles such as plant diversity, fibre, fermented foods, and polyphenols.

Sources: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf

Last updated: 2026-04-14

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
These recipes are provided by ZOE as practical examples of gut-friendly eating (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf). Each recipe combines multiple components of a healthy gut diet — [[plant-diversity]], [[dietary-fibre]], [[fermented-foods]], and [[polyphenols]].

All recipes are simple, with a preparation time of 5–15 minutes.

docs.title: Recipe 1: Aubergine with Harissa Yoghurt and Roasted Chickpeas
docs.text:
Prep time: 5 min | Total time: 35 min | Serves: 2

Gut-health features: plant diversity (aubergine, tomatoes, garlic, parsley, spring onion, sesame seeds, pomegranate), fermented food (Greek yoghurt), polyphenols (olive oil, pomegranate)

Ingredients: 1 aubergine, 1 tin chickpeas (400 g), 2 handfuls cherry tomatoes, 2 tsp harissa, 4 cloves garlic, 4 tbsp Greek yoghurt, 2 tbsp tahini, 2 tbsp extra virgin olive oil, 1 tbsp pomegranate seeds, 2 spring onions, 1 handful parsley, 1 tbsp sesame seeds, salt and pepper.

Method: Toss aubergine, chickpeas, and tomatoes in harissa and olive oil; roast at 190°C for 30 min. Mix remaining harissa with yoghurt and roasted garlic. Mix tahini with water for a dressing. Serve aubergine and chickpeas over yoghurt, drizzled with tahini and topped with pomegranate, spring onion, parsley, and sesame seeds.

docs.title: Recipe 2: Creamy Courgette Butter Beans with Herby Tomato Salsa
docs.text:
Prep time: 5 min | Total time: 25 min | Serves: 2

Gut-health features: plant diversity (courgette, butter beans, tomatoes, dill, parsley, basil, chilli, pumpkin and sunflower seeds), fibre (butter beans), polyphenols (olive oil, tomatoes)

Ingredients: 1 courgette, 2 shallots, 1 garlic clove, 2 tins butter beans (400 g each), 1 lemon, 10 cherry tomatoes, 1 tbsp capers, fresh dill, parsley and basil, 1 red chilli, 1 tbsp each pumpkin and sunflower seeds (toasted), extra virgin olive oil, salt and pepper.

Method: Sauté shallots, add thinly sliced courgette, then garlic and butter beans with their liquid. Stir in lemon zest and simmer. Combine quartered tomatoes, capers, herbs, lemon, and olive oil for salsa. Spoon salsa over beans and top with toasted seeds and chilli.

docs.title: Recipe 3: Roasted Carrots with Tahini Yoghurt
docs.text:
Prep time: 5 min | Total time: 35 min | Serves: 2

Gut-health features: plant diversity (carrots, garlic, shallot, carrot tops), fermented food (Greek yoghurt), polyphenols (olive oil)

Ingredients: 1 bunch carrots with tops, 1 head of garlic, 1 shallot, 2 tbsp tahini, 150 g Greek yoghurt, 2 tbsp apple cider vinegar, extra virgin olive oil, salt and pepper.

Method: Roast carrots and garlic in olive oil at 180°C for 20–30 min. Finely chop shallot and carrot tops with apple cider vinegar and olive oil for a dressing. Mix tahini, yoghurt, and roasted garlic for the base. Serve carrots over tahini yoghurt with carrot top dressing.

docs.title: Recipe 4: Green Goddess Chickpea Sandwich
docs.text:
Prep time: 10 min | Total time: 10 min | Serves: 1

Gut-health features: plant diversity (avocado, parsley, dill, spring onions, chickpeas), fermented food (kefir), fibre (chickpeas)

Ingredients: 1/4 avocado, 3 tbsp kefir, juice of 1/2 lemon, 1 garlic clove, handful each of parsley and dill, a splash of vinegar, 1 tin chickpeas (400 g), 2 spring onions, salt and pepper.

Method: Blend avocado, kefir, lemon juice, garlic, vinegar, parsley, and dill until smooth. Pour dressing over chickpeas and mash together. Stir in additional herbs and spring onions. Use as a sandwich filling or salad topping.

docs.title: Recipe 5: Seedy Crackers with Beetroot Hummus
docs.text:
Prep time: 15 min | Total time: 1 hr 15 min | Serves: 4

Gut-health features: plant diversity (beetroot, chickpeas, flaxseed, chia seeds, mixed seeds), fibre (seeds, chickpeas), polyphenols (beetroot, lemon)

Ingredients (crackers): 80 g ground flaxseed, 3 tbsp chia seeds, 100 g mixed seeds, 170 ml water, pinch of salt. Ingredients (hummus): 1 tin chickpeas (400 g), 1 beetroot (pre-cooked), 2 tbsp tahini, juice of 1 lemon, 1 garlic clove, a few ice cubes, salt.

Method: Mix seeds with water, rest 15 min, roll thin between parchment, bake at 150°C for 1 hour. Cool before breaking into crackers. Blend all hummus ingredients until smooth. Serve crackers with beetroot hummus.

docs.title: Gut Health Made Easy (ZOE, 2024)
docs.text:
Summary: A practical guide from ZOE outlining five evidence-informed dietary steps to improve gut health, with recipes, a shopping list, and a glossary of key terms.

Sources: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf

Last updated: 2026-04-14

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This guide was produced by ZOE, a nutrition science company. It aims to make gut health accessible to a general audience by distilling research into simple, actionable steps. The guide states that good nutrition is about balance, not perfection.

docs.title: The Five Steps
docs.text:
The guide identifies five basic building blocks for a gut-friendly diet:

  1. Eat 30 different plants a week — includes herbs, spices, fruits, vegetables, canned and frozen options, seeds, and nuts. See [[plant-diversity]].
  2. Try more colourful plants — add a new colour to meals, include fruit in savoury dishes, cook with extra virgin olive oil. See [[polyphenols]].
  3. Increase your fibre intake — make high-fibre swaps, use pulses and legumes, batch cook grains, keep skins on vegetables. See [[dietary-fibre]].
  4. Add more fermented foods — small amounts daily, e.g. sauerkraut, kefir, yoghurt, fermented cheese. See [[fermented-foods]].
  5. Limit ultra-processed foods — swap snacks, switch breakfast cereal for yoghurt and fruit, make homemade sauces. See [[ultra-processed-foods]].

docs.title: Recipes Included
docs.text:
Five gut-friendly recipes are provided. See [[gut-friendly-recipes]] for full details.

  • Aubergine with harissa yoghurt and roasted chickpeas
  • Creamy courgette butter beans with herby tomato salsa
  • Roasted carrots with tahini yoghurt
  • Green goddess chickpea sandwich
  • Seedy crackers with beetroot hummus

docs.title: Shopping List
docs.text: A gut-friendly shopping list covers: whole grains, beans and legumes, nuts and seeds, vegetables, fruit, herbs, spices, flavour boosters (garlic, ginger, onion, lemon), fermented foods (kefir, kimchi, miso, sauerkraut, yoghurt, kombucha), and other staples (extra virgin olive oil, apple cider vinegar, harissa).

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text:
The guide defines these terms (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf):

  • Digestive system: organs responsible for breaking down food, absorbing nutrients, and eliminating waste.
  • Fermented food: food changed by beneficial bacteria or yeasts that break down starches and sugars.
  • Fibre: a carbohydrate in plant foods that the body cannot digest; supports gut microbiome and bowel regularity.
  • Gut health: the well-being of the digestive system, including gut lining, microbiome composition, and ability to digest and absorb nutrients.
  • Gut microbiome: the community of trillions of bacteria and other microbes in the digestive system.
  • Plant diversity: the variety of plants eaten, including fruits, vegetables, whole grains, legumes, nuts, seeds, herbs, and spices.
  • Polyphenols: compounds in plants that provide colour, offer health benefits, and may promote beneficial gut bacteria.
  • Prebiotics: types of fibre that feed beneficial bacteria; found in oats, garlic, onions, leeks, and asparagus.
  • Probiotic foods: foods containing live microorganisms (bacteria or yeasts) that provide health benefits.
  • Ultra-processed foods (UPFs): manufactured products with many artificial ingredients and additives; typically high in sugar, salt, and unhealthy fats.

docs.text:

  • [[plant-diversity]]
  • [[dietary-fibre]]
  • [[fermented-foods]]
  • [[ultra-processed-foods]]
  • [[polyphenols]]
  • [[gut-friendly-recipes]]

docs.title: Gut Health Made Easy — UK Edition (ZOE, 2025)
docs.text:
Summary: The UK version of ZOE's "Gut Health Made Easy" guide. Content is nearly identical to the 2024 US edition but includes a more comprehensive gut-friendly shopping list with additional plant varieties.

Sources: 250820 uk-gut-health-guide.pdf

Last updated: 2026-04-15

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
This is the UK-market edition of ZOE's gut health guide. It covers the same five dietary steps, five recipes, and glossary as the US edition (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf). The primary difference is an expanded shopping list and UK conventions (Celsius temperatures, British spelling such as "courgette", "fibre").

For full content details, see [[gut-health-guide-zoe-2024]].

docs.title: Expanded Shopping List
docs.text:
The UK edition shopping list is more comprehensive than the US edition. Additional items not in the US list include (source: 250820 uk-gut-health-guide.pdf):

Whole grains: Amaranth, Freekeh, Pasta (wholemeal, brown rice, chickpea, lentil), Sorghum, Teff, Wild rice

Beans and legumes: Black-eyed peas, Broad beans, Haricot beans, Pinto beans, Red kidney beans

Vegetables: Avocado, Brussels sprouts, Celery, Courgette, Cucumber, Lettuce, Purple cabbage, Radish, Sweetcorn

Fruit: Grapes (green and red), Melon (cantaloupe, honeydew), Peach, Pear, Pineapple, Plum

Nuts and seeds: Cashews, Hemp seeds, Nut butters (peanut, almond, cashew), Pine nuts, Pistachios, Walnuts

Fermented foods: Pickles, Tempeh (in addition to the US list)

Flavour boosters: Capers, Jalapeño, Lime, Roasted peppers, Sun-dried tomatoes

Herbs: Chives, Oregano, Sage

Spices: Cayenne pepper, Chilli powder, Cloves, Nutmeg

This expanded list is useful for seniors aiming to reach 30 different plants per week, as it highlights many convenient options (canned, frozen, or shelf-stable).

docs.title: Recipes
docs.text: The five recipes are identical to the US edition. See [[gut-friendly-recipes]] for details.

docs.title: Key difference from US edition
docs.text: The US edition notes temperatures in Fahrenheit; the UK edition uses Celsius. Recipe ingredients and methods are otherwise the same.

docs.title: Gut Microbiome
docs.text:
Summary: The community of trillions of bacteria and other microbes living in the digestive system, which plays a crucial role in overall health.

Sources: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf

Last updated: 2026-04-14

docs.title: What is the gut microbiome?
docs.text:
The gut microbiome is the community of trillions of bacteria and other microbes that live in the digestive system (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf). It plays a crucial role in overall health, including digestion, immune function, and nutrient absorption.

The microbiome lives within the digestive system and is directly influenced by what we eat. A diverse and well-nourished microbiome is associated with better gut health outcomes.

docs.title: Why it matters for seniors
docs.text: Gut microbiome diversity tends to decrease with age. Supporting the microbiome through diet is therefore particularly relevant for adults over 75. The five steps in the ZOE guide are specifically aimed at helping the microbiome thrive.

docs.title: How to support the gut microbiome
docs.text:
The ZOE guide identifies several dietary approaches that benefit the gut microbiome (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf):

  • Eating a wide variety of plants — see [[plant-diversity]]
  • Consuming adequate [[dietary-fibre]], including prebiotics
  • Eating [[fermented-foods]] containing live beneficial bacteria (probiotic foods)
  • Consuming colourful plants rich in [[polyphenols]]
  • Limiting [[ultra-processed-foods]], which are associated with negative gut health effects

docs.title: Key definitions
docs.text:
• Gut health: The well-being of the digestive system, including the gut lining, microbiome composition, and the ability to digest and absorb nutrients.
  • Prebiotics: Special types of fibre that help beneficial bacteria grow. Found in oats, garlic, onions, leeks, and asparagus.
  • Probiotic foods: Foods containing live microorganisms — typically bacteria or yeasts — that provide health benefits when eaten in the right amounts.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• [[gut-health-guide-zoe-2024]]
  • [[plant-diversity]]
  • [[dietary-fibre]]
  • [[fermented-foods]]
  • [[polyphenols]]
  • [[ultra-processed-foods]]

docs.title: Wiki Log
docs.text: Append-only record of all wiki operations.

docs.title: 2026-04-14 — Initial ingest: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf
docs.text:
Source: ZOE "Gut Health Made Easy" guide (2024)

Pages created: - gut-health-guide-zoe-2024.md — source summary - gut-microbiome.md — concept page - plant-diversity.md — concept page - dietary-fibre.md — concept page - fermented-foods.md — concept page - polyphenols.md — concept page - ultra-processed-foods.md — concept page - gut-friendly-recipes.md — concept page

Pages updated: - index.md — created with all new pages listed - log.md — created (this file)

Notes: First source ingested into the wiki. Guide covers five dietary steps for gut health, five recipes, a gut-friendly shopping list, and a glossary. Particularly relevant for seniors over 75 due to practical, food-based advice.

docs.title: 2026-04-14 — Lint pass
docs.text:
Issues found and fixed: 1. gut-microbiome.md: Removed broken link [[digestive system]] — no such page exists; converted to plain text. 2. gut-microbiome.md: Removed broken link [[prebiotics]] — no such page exists; prebiotics content is covered within dietary-fibre.md. 3. gut-health-guide-zoe-2024.md: Corrected "Four gut-friendly recipes" to "Five gut-friendly recipes". 4. fermented-foods.md: Added missing [[polyphenols]] and [[plant-diversity]] to Related pages. 5. gut-friendly-recipes.md: Removed apple cider vinegar from polyphenol sources in Recipe 3 — it is a flavour booster, not a polyphenol source.

Issues flagged (not yet fixed — needs new source): - polyphenols.md: Claim that dark chocolate and green tea are polyphenol sources is marked as needing verification. Not supported by this source. - [[prebiotics]] is linked from dietary-fibre.md but has no dedicated page. Consider creating one when a suitable source is ingested.

docs.title: 2026-04-15 — Ingest: 250820 uk-gut-health-guide.pdf
docs.text:
Source: ZOE "Gut Health Made Easy" — UK edition (2025)

Pages created: - gut-health-guide-zoe-uk.md — source summary; notes UK edition differs mainly in expanded shopping list

Pages updated: - index.md — added new source summary page

Notes: Content nearly identical to the already-ingested US edition. The UK shopping list adds: Amaranth, Freekeh, Teff, Sorghum, Wild rice, Black-eyed peas, Broad beans, Haricot beans, Pinto beans, Red kidney beans, Tempeh, Pickles, Jalapeño, and more. No new concepts required.

docs.title: 2026-04-15 — Ingest: 251030 Breakfast 1761216573-zoe_better_breakfast_guide_us.pdf
docs.text:
Source: ZOE "Better Breakfast Guide" — US edition (October 2025)

Pages created: - better-breakfast-guide-zoe.md — source summary with key statistics, five breakfast swaps, recipe list, and expert advice - breakfast-recipes.md — concept page with full details of six breakfast recipes - blood-sugar.md — new concept page: blood sugar stability, breakfast composition, and relevance for seniors

Pages updated: - dietary-fibre.md — added resistant starch section; added fibre-at-breakfast statistics; updated Sources and Related pages; updated Last updated date - index.md — added three new pages

Key new concepts: - Resistant starch (black beans, cooled potatoes/rice, oats) — ferments in large intestine, feeding gut bacteria - Blood sugar stability as a breakfast-specific concern - Flavonoids (subclass of polyphenols) found in peppers

Key statistics (from ZOE survey, source: 251030 Breakfast guide): - Only 6% of people get enough fibre at breakfast - Only 16% felt energetic after eating breakfast - 55% were hungry again within three hours

Notes: This is the most practically useful source yet for seniors — overnight recipes (chia pudding, Bircher muesli) require minimal morning effort. High-protein breakfasts align with muscle-maintenance needs in adults over 75.

docs.title: 2026-04-14 — Ingest: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md
docs.text:
Source: ZOE article by Tim Newman, reviewed by Dr Federica Amati PhD MPH RNutr. Published 2025-04-01. URL: https://zoe.com/learn/not-all-upfs-are-equal-health

Pages created: - are-all-upfs-equally-bad.md — source summary - nova-classification.md — concept page (NOVA food classification system) - zoe-processed-food-risk-scale.md — concept page (ZOE's five-tier risk scale)

Pages updated: - ultra-processed-foods.md — added nuance section: not all UPFs equally harmful; added higher/lower risk UPF lists; updated Related pages; updated Sources field - index.md — added three new pages

Key contradiction noted: The ZOE Gut Health Guide 2024 frames UPFs broadly negatively. The 2025 article explicitly states some UPFs are neutral or beneficial. Both sources agree on limiting the highest-risk categories. Contradiction documented in are-all-upfs-equally-bad.md and addressed in ultra-processed-foods.md.

docs.title: NOVA Classification
docs.text:
Summary: A food classification system that groups foods into four categories based on the nature and extent of industrial processing. Widely used in research but limited in distinguishing health impact within ultra-processed foods.

Sources: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md

Last updated: 2026-04-14

docs.title: What is NOVA?
docs.text:
NOVA is a food classification framework, first outlined in 1990, that classifies "all foods according to the nature, extent, and purposes of the industrial processes they undergo" (source: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md).

It is widely used by researchers studying the relationship between food processing and health.

docs.title: The four groups
docs.text:
• Group 1 — Unprocessed or minimally processed foods: foods in their natural state, or foods altered only to make them safer or easier to store (e.g. fresh vegetables, plain yoghurt, eggs, fresh meat).
  • Group 2 — Processed culinary ingredients: substances extracted from Group 1 foods and used in cooking (e.g. oils, butter, flour, sugar, salt).
  • Group 3 — Processed foods: foods made by combining Groups 1 and 2, typically with salt, sugar, or oil added (e.g. tinned vegetables, cured meats, cheese, freshly baked bread).
  • Group 4 — Ultra-processed foods (UPFs): industrial formulations using ingredients rarely found in home kitchens, including artificial colours, emulsifiers, flavourings, and preservatives.

docs.title: Limitations of NOVA
docs.text:
The article identifies two key problems with NOVA (source: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md):

  1. Ambiguity: Scientists and nutritionists sometimes disagree on how to categorise specific foods.
  2. Blunt instrument for health: Some Group 4 foods are neutral or even beneficial for health, making NOVA difficult to apply in daily life. The system was designed to classify processing level, not health impact.

Large population studies consistently show that high overall UPF consumption is linked to poorer outcomes — but when UPF types are separated, the picture becomes more complex. See [[are-all-upfs-equally-bad]].

docs.title: Alternative: ZOE's Processed Food Risk Scale
docs.text: ZOE has developed the [[zoe-processed-food-risk-scale]] to address NOVA's limitations. It evaluates foods on energy density, hyperpalatability, energy intake rate, and the type and number of additives — giving a more direct measure of likely health impact.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• [[ultra-processed-foods]]
  • [[are-all-upfs-equally-bad]]
  • [[zoe-processed-food-risk-scale]]
  • [[gut-microbiome]]

docs.title: Plant Diversity
docs.text:
Summary: The variety of plant foods eaten in the diet, including fruits, vegetables, whole grains, legumes, nuts, seeds, herbs, and spices. Eating 30 different plants per week is a key recommendation for gut health.

Sources: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf

Last updated: 2026-04-14

docs.title: What is plant diversity?
docs.text:
Plant diversity refers to the variety of plants eaten in the diet. This includes fruits, vegetables, whole grains, legumes, nuts, seeds, herbs, and spices (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf).

A diverse range of plant foods feeds a wider variety of beneficial gut bacteria, which supports a healthy [[gut-microbiome]].

docs.title: The 30-plants-a-week target
docs.text:
The ZOE guide recommends eating 30 different plants per week as a key step for gut health (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf). This does not mean 30 large servings — herbs, spices, seeds, and nuts all count.

Practical tips from the guide: - Add different herbs and spices to meals - Try a new fruit or vegetable every week - Explore canned and frozen options (these count equally) - Make a mix of seeds and nuts to add to meals

docs.title: Why variety matters
docs.text: Different plants contain different types of [[dietary-fibre]] and [[polyphenols]]. Eating a wide range gives gut bacteria more diverse "fuel", which supports a richer and more resilient microbiome.

docs.title: Gut-friendly shopping list (plants)
docs.text:
The guide provides a comprehensive list. Key categories include (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf):

  • Whole grains: oats, brown rice, quinoa, rye, spelt, farro, buckwheat, bulgur wheat, millet, barley, and others
  • Beans and legumes: chickpeas, lentils, butter beans, black beans, cannellini beans, edamame, and others
  • Vegetables: artichoke, asparagus, aubergine, beetroot, broccoli, carrots, cauliflower, kale, pumpkin, spinach, sweet potato, and others
  • Fruit: apple, banana, blueberries, kiwi, mango, orange, pomegranate, raspberries, strawberries, and others
  • Herbs: basil, coriander, dill, mint, parsley, rosemary, thyme, and others
  • Spices: cinnamon, cumin, turmeric, cardamom, paprika, and others

docs.title: Practical tips for seniors
docs.text: Canned and frozen vegetables and legumes count towards the 30 plants and require less preparation, making them a convenient option.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• [[gut-health-guide-zoe-2024]]
  • [[gut-microbiome]]
  • [[dietary-fibre]]
  • [[polyphenols]]
  • [[gut-friendly-recipes]]

docs.title: Polyphenols
docs.text:
Summary: A group of compounds found in plants that provide colour, offer health benefits, and may promote the growth of beneficial gut bacteria.

Sources: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf

Last updated: 2026-04-14

docs.title: What are polyphenols?
docs.text:
Polyphenols are a group of compounds found in plants that provide colour, offer health benefits, and may promote the growth of beneficial gut bacteria (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf).

They are the reason colourful fruits and vegetables are particularly good for gut health. Different colours in plants typically signal different types of polyphenols, which is why eating a variety of colourful plants is recommended.

docs.title: Polyphenols and the gut microbiome
docs.text: Polyphenols act as a food source for beneficial gut bacteria, supporting a healthy [[gut-microbiome]]. They also contribute to [[plant-diversity]] benefits beyond [[dietary-fibre]] alone.

docs.title: How to get more polyphenols
docs.text:
The ZOE guide recommends (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf):

  • Add a new colour to one of your meals each day
  • Include a portion of fruit in a savoury meal
  • Buy a different coloured version of a familiar vegetable (e.g. purple carrots, red cabbage)
  • Cook or dress food with extra virgin olive oil, which is also rich in polyphenols

docs.title: Good sources of polyphenols
docs.text:
From the ZOE shopping list and recipes (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf):

  • Berries (blueberries, raspberries, strawberries)
  • Pomegranate
  • Red and purple vegetables (beetroot, red cabbage, aubergine, purple carrots)
  • Dark leafy greens (kale, spinach)
  • Extra virgin olive oil
  • Herbs and spices (turmeric, cinnamon, oregano, rosemary)
  • Dark chocolate and green tea (not mentioned in this guide but well established in literature — needs verification)

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• [[gut-health-guide-zoe-2024]]
  • [[gut-microbiome]]
  • [[plant-diversity]]
  • [[dietary-fibre]]
  • [[gut-friendly-recipes]]

docs.title: Ultra-Processed Foods
docs.text:
Summary: Manufactured food products that contain many artificial ingredients and additives. Evidence suggests they may negatively impact gut health and should be limited.

Sources: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf; Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md

Last updated: 2026-04-14

docs.title: What are ultra-processed foods?
docs.text:
Ultra-processed foods (UPFs) are manufactured products that often contain many artificial ingredients and additives (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf). They are typically high in sugar, salt, and unhealthy fats. Evidence suggests they may negatively impact gut health.

Examples include many packaged snacks, breakfast cereals, ready meals, fizzy drinks, and jarred sauces.

docs.title: Why limit them?
docs.text: UPFs are associated with a less healthy [[gut-microbiome]]. Their artificial ingredients and additives may disrupt the gut environment. They also tend to displace more nutritious, whole foods that feed beneficial bacteria — such as [[plant-diversity]] foods and [[dietary-fibre]].

docs.title: Practical ways to reduce UPFs
docs.text:
The ZOE guide recommends these simple swaps (source: 241101 Gut-Health-Guide-29-12-23.pdf):

  • Swap ultra-processed snacks for less processed ones (e.g. nuts, fruit)
  • Switch breakfast cereal for yoghurt and fruit
  • Make yourself a packed lunch if going out, rather than buying processed options
  • Try homemade sauce instead of one from a jar

docs.title: Not all UPFs are equally harmful
docs.text:
A 2025 ZOE article (source: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md) adds important nuance: research from studies of 200,000–300,000+ people shows that UPF categories behave very differently.

Higher-risk UPFs (consistently linked to cardiovascular disease and type 2 diabetes): - Sugar-sweetened and artificially-sweetened drinks - Processed meats and animal-based products - Ready-to-eat mixed dishes and sauces/condiments

Lower-risk or neutral UPFs (linked to no increased risk, or even reduced risk, in multiple large studies): - Wholegrain and dark breads - Breakfast cereals - Yoghurt and dairy-based desserts - Some savoury snacks (e.g. popcorn) - Plant-based alternatives

Lower-risk UPFs tend to contain [[dietary-fibre]], minerals, and [[polyphenols]], and are less likely to be highly energy-dense or hyperpalatable (source: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md).

The [[nova-classification]] system groups all these together as "Group 4", which is why blanket UPF advice can be misleading. ZOE's [[zoe-processed-food-risk-scale]] offers a more precise five-tier alternative.

docs.title: Notes for seniors
docs.text:
Both sources agree that balance is the goal, not perfection. ZOE's 80/20 guidance suggests 80% whole foods and 20% more convenient options as a realistic long-term target (source: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md). Small, gradual changes — especially cutting the highest-risk items consumed most often — are a realistic approach.

For seniors with reduced cooking capacity, it is reassuring that some convenient processed foods (wholegrain bread, yoghurt, cereals) carry low risk and can remain in the diet.

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• [[gut-health-guide-zoe-2024]]
  • [[are-all-upfs-equally-bad]]
  • [[nova-classification]]
  • [[zoe-processed-food-risk-scale]]
  • [[gut-microbiome]]
  • [[dietary-fibre]]
  • [[plant-diversity]]
  • [[fermented-foods]]

docs.title: ZOE Processed Food Risk Scale
docs.text:
Summary: A five-tier food classification system developed by ZOE that rates processed foods by their likely health impact, accounting for energy density, hyperpalatability, and additive type — rather than processing level alone.

Sources: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md

Last updated: 2026-04-14

docs.title: What is it?
docs.text: The ZOE Processed Food Risk Scale is a classification system developed by ZOE to improve on the [[nova-classification]] system (source: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md). Rather than categorising foods by how much they are processed, it rates them by their likely long-term health impact when consumed regularly.

docs.title: What it measures
docs.text:
The scale takes four factors into account (source: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md):

  • Energy density: how many calories per gram of food.
  • Energy intake rate: how quickly those calories are consumed.
  • Hyperpalatability: whether the food is designed to encourage over-eating.
  • Non-culinary additives: artificial colours, sweeteners, and preservatives — assessed against rulings by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA) and the US Food and Drug Administration (FDA).

In ZOE's PREDICT study data (891 people, aged 34–58), energy-dense and hyperpalatable foods were found to make up a large share of typical diets: - 56% of foods were energy-dense. - 52% were hyperpalatable. - 38% were both energy-dense and hyperpalatable.

docs.title: The five tiers
docs.text: Tier Meaning Unprocessed Food in or close to its original form; processing does not affect healthiness. No Risk Minimal processing; poses no risk to health. Low Risk Processing has no or very low impact on health. Medium Risk Processing may make the food less healthy; moderate impact if consumed regularly over the long term. Highest Risk Processing makes the food less healthy; high impact if consumed regularly over the long term.

docs.title: Why it matters for seniors
docs.text: For adults over 75, the practical value of this scale is in distinguishing which processed foods are worth limiting. Rather than avoiding all convenience foods (which may be helpful for those with reduced cooking capacity), the scale allows a more targeted approach: prioritise cutting Highest Risk items such as processed meats and sweetened drinks, while being more relaxed about lower-risk processed options such as wholegrain bread or yoghurt.

docs.title: ZOE's 80/20 guidance
docs.text: ZOE recommends aiming for 80% whole foods and 20% more convenient options as a realistic long-term balance (source: Are All UPFs Equally Bad For Health_.md).

docs.title: Related pages
docs.text:
• [[ultra-processed-foods]]
  • [[nova-classification]]
  • [[are-all-upfs-equally-bad]]
  • [[dietary-fibre]]
  • [[gut-microbiome]]
